title: Carburetor CFM Calculator
tagline: Calculate the required cubic feet per minute (CFM) for optimal engine performance based on displacement, RPM, and volumetric efficiency.
intro: Enter your engine displacement, maximum RPM, and volumetric efficiency to find the ideal carburetor CFM rating.

error.invalid: Please enter valid positive values for displacement, RPM, and volumetric efficiency (1–100%).

about.heading: About the Carburetor CFM Calculator
about.body:
CFM, or Cubic Feet per Minute, is the measurement of air flow capacity that a carburetor can deliver to an engine. It is one of the most critical specifications when selecting a carburetor for your engine build or upgrade. Choosing the correct CFM rating ensures your engine receives the optimal amount of air-fuel mixture at the right time — too little CFM restricts maximum power output, while too much CFM can cause poor low-end performance, hesitation, and reduced fuel economy.

The carburetor CFM requirement is calculated using a straightforward formula derived from fundamental engine airflow principles: CFM = (Engine Displacement × RPM × Volumetric Efficiency) / 3456. The constant 3456 accounts for unit conversion and the fact that each cylinder fires once every two crankshaft revolutions in a four-stroke engine. Engine displacement is measured in cubic inches, RPM refers to the maximum operating speed where peak power is desired, and volumetric efficiency expresses how completely the engine fills each cylinder with the air-fuel charge during intake.

Volumetric efficiency is arguably the most important variable in this calculation. A perfectly sealed, frictionless engine with ideal valve timing would achieve 100% volumetric efficiency, but real-world engines fall short of this ideal. Stock production engines typically achieve 75–85% volumetric efficiency at their peak power RPM. Performance engines with ported heads, aggressive camshaft profiles, and tuned intake manifolds can reach 90–95%. Supercharged or turbocharged engines may exceed 100% volumetric efficiency because forced induction packs more air into the cylinder than its displacement would naturally allow.

Carburetors are typically available in ranges from about 200 CFM for small four-cylinder engines up to 1050 CFM or more for large-displacement, high-performance V8 engines. Common carburetor sizes include 390 CFM, 450 CFM, 600 CFM, 650 CFM, 750 CFM, 850 CFM, and 950 CFM. A popular misconception is that bigger is always better — installing an oversized carburetor on a mild engine results in sluggish throttle response, poor idle quality, and poor low-speed drivability because the air velocity through the venturi is too low to atomize fuel properly.

Different applications have different priorities. A street-driven daily driver benefits from a smaller, correctly sized carburetor that promotes good fuel atomization and crisp throttle response across the full RPM range. A dedicated drag racing engine may tolerate a slightly larger carburetor to ensure maximum airflow at peak RPM. Engines used in road racing or autocross must balance top-end airflow with strong mid-range torque.

Altitude also affects carburetor sizing. At higher elevations, air density decreases, reducing the effective CFM available. Engines operated consistently at high altitudes may benefit from a slightly larger carburetor to compensate for the thinner air. Temperature similarly affects air density — hot days reduce the density of the incoming air charge, slightly reducing effective volumetric efficiency and engine output.

This calculator gives you the theoretical ideal CFM requirement based on your inputs. In practice, many experienced engine builders round up to the next available carburetor size and rely on proper jetting and tuning to optimize the air-fuel ratio across the operating range.

examples.heading: Carburetor CFM Examples
examples.intro: Common engine configurations and their calculated CFM requirements.

items.note: Typical stock Chevrolet 350 small block for street use. A 450 CFM carburetor is the ideal match.
High-performance big block with ported heads. A 750–800 CFM carburetor suits this build well.
Ford 302 small block for street/strip. A 390–450 CFM carburetor provides excellent drivability.
Race-built big block stroker. Requires a 950 CFM carburetor for maximum wide-open-throttle airflow.

howto.heading: How to Use the Carburetor CFM Calculator
steps: Enter your engine's total displacement in cubic inches. Common examples are 302, 350, 383, 454, or 496 cubic inches.
Enter the maximum RPM where you want peak performance — typically the engine's redline or the RPM of peak power on the dyno.
Enter the volumetric efficiency as a percentage. Use 75–85% for stock engines, 85–93% for performance engines with upgraded heads and camshaft.
Optionally enter the number of cylinders. While not used in the CFM formula directly, it helps contextualize the result.
Click Calculate. The result shows the minimum CFM your carburetor must flow. Round up to the nearest available carburetor size.

faq.heading: Carburetor CFM FAQ
faq.q1: What does CFM mean for a carburetor?
faq.q1_a: CFM stands for Cubic Feet per Minute and represents the maximum volume of air the carburetor can flow at a given pressure differential. It is the primary specification used to match a carburetor's capacity to an engine's airflow demand. A carburetor rated at 600 CFM can flow 600 cubic feet of air per minute at a 1.5-inch water pressure drop, which is the industry-standard test condition.
faq.q2: What volumetric efficiency should I use for my engine?
faq.q2_a: Stock production engines with factory heads, camshaft, and intake manifold typically run 75–85% volumetric efficiency at peak power RPM. Performance engines with ported cylinder heads, an aggressive camshaft, and a high-rise intake manifold can achieve 88–93%. Fully race-prepared engines with optimized port work, large-valve heads, and a purpose-built intake may reach 95–100%. When in doubt, use 80% for a mild street engine.
faq.q3: Can a carburetor be too large for an engine?
faq.q3_a: Yes, and this is a very common mistake. An oversized carburetor creates low air velocity through the venturi bore, which fails to atomize fuel effectively. The result is a rich, wet mixture at low throttle openings, causing poor idle quality, hesitation under light acceleration, increased fuel consumption, and a flat mid-range. The engine may still make good peak power, but everyday drivability suffers considerably.
faq.q4: Does the formula change for two-barrel versus four-barrel carburetors?
faq.q4_a: No — the CFM formula calculates total airflow required by the engine regardless of carburetor configuration. Whether you use a single two-barrel, a single four-barrel, or a multi-carburetor setup, the total airflow demand is the same. The formula result tells you the total CFM needed; you then choose a carburetor configuration that meets that total.
faq.q5: How does altitude affect carburetor sizing?
faq.q5_a: At higher altitudes, air density is lower, meaning each cubic foot of air contains less mass of oxygen than at sea level. This effectively reduces the engine's power output and may cause a rich condition if the carburetor is jetted for sea level. At altitudes above 2000 feet, you may need to re-jet the carburetor leaner. Some builders at high altitude also choose a slightly larger carburetor to maintain adequate airflow for the thinner air.
faq.q6: What is the constant 3456 in the formula?
faq.q6_a: The constant 3456 is a unit conversion factor derived from the four-stroke engine cycle. Each cylinder draws air once every two crankshaft revolutions, and there are 1728 cubic inches in one cubic foot. The number 3456 = 1728 × 2, combining both factors. Dividing by 3456 converts the result from cubic inches per minute into cubic feet per minute, giving you the CFM figure directly.
